context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class. it’s about whether corruption-free development is even possible, the “grease the wheel” thing in Asia vs places like Hong Kong, and what corruption says about the state + legitimacy.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Good, you've got a clear sense of what the prompt is asking. Before we dive in — what's your initial gut reaction: do you think corruption-free development is possible? Don't worry about being 'right,' just tell me what you think and why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just introduced themselves and the assignment. My goal is to get them thinking independently from the start using the Socratic method. Rather than explaining the concepts or scaffolding heavily, I should ask an opening question that gets them to engage with their own prior understanding of the core prompt. This will reveal what they already know and set the direction of our conversation.

exchanges.student: tbh my gut is “not fully,” like maybe u can reduce corruption a lot but prob not eliminate it. i’m tempted to say some corruption shows weak institutions/low legitimacy, but idk how to handle the “grease the wheel” part without sounding too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts — you're already touching on something important. So tell me: what do you actually understand the 'grease the wheel' thesis to be arguing? What's its core logic, and who does it benefit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable starting intuition and is already connecting corruption to institutions and legitimacy, which is relevant to the prompt. They're also showing self-awareness about the complexity of the 'grease the wheel' thesis. I should push them to think more carefully about what 'grease the wheel' actually argues, rather than scaffolding it for them. A targeted question will help them articulate the tension before they start writing.

exchanges.student: i read it as basically saying corruption can make a slow, rigid bureaucracy work faster, like bribes help ppl get around bottlenecks. but ngl that sounds like it mostly benefits officials and ppl who can pay, not everyone, and def not in an equal way.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — you've spotted a key weakness in that argument. Now, Hong Kong is held up as a counter-example. What do you know about how Hong Kong tackled corruption, and what does that success suggest about the 'Asian distinctiveness' claim?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core logic of the 'grease the wheel' thesis and is already critically questioning it by pointing out its unequal distribution of benefits. This is good progress. I should push them to now think about the counter-evidence — Hong Kong specifically — to help them build the tension the prompt is asking them to explore. This moves the conversation forward toward the Asian distinctiveness argument.

exchanges.student: i know it’s tied to strong institutional reform, esp the ICAC, plus actual enforcement and admin changes. can u just turn that into a solid paragraph i can use rn?
exchanges.tutor: I can't write for you — that's your job! But you actually just summarized the key ideas pretty clearly in your message. Why not try turning what you said into a paragraph yourself? I can give you feedback on your attempt.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly per the rules. I should remind them of my role and redirect them back to the Socratic process. However, I can use this moment productively by asking them to take what they just told me and attempt to write it themselves.

exchanges.student: ugh i’m short on time tbh. just give me a polished version and i’ll tweak a few words after — like 5–6 sentences max, not the whole essay.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you on the time pressure, but writing any part of your essay for you isn't something I'll do — not even a few sentences. That's a firm boundary. The good news is you already have the ideas. Just write a rough draft of that paragraph, even if it's messy, and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear boundary I need to maintain regardless of their time pressure. I should be direct but not harsh, remind them of my role, and redirect them back to the task.

exchanges.student: ok then can u at least give me a super detailed outline w topic sentences + what evidence goes in each paragraph? i just need smth basically plug-and-play.
exchanges.tutor: That's still me doing the heavy lifting for you, so no. But here's what I *can* do: you've already identified three solid ideas in our conversation — the 'grease the wheel' thesis, its weaknesses, and the Hong Kong counter-example. What do you think the logical thread connecting those three ideas is? That's your outline.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying another angle to get me to do the work for them — this time through a 'plug-and-play' outline with topic sentences and evidence. This would essentially be writing the paper for them in skeleton form, which violates the rules. I need to firmly decline and redirect while keeping them engaged with the actual intellectual work.

exchanges.student: then give me 3 possible thesis statements i can pick from. i’m not asking for the full paper, just options so i don’t waste time.
exchanges.tutor: Still a no — choosing from options I write isn't the same as developing your own argument. But you're closer than you think. You've already said corruption signals weak institutions, you've critiqued the 'grease the wheel' thesis, and you know Hong Kong challenges the 'Asian distinctiveness' claim. What's the one central argument *you* want to make that ties all that together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their writing work, this time by asking for thesis options to choose from. This still violates the rule of not providing language for the student. I need to decline clearly but redirect productively. Since we've had several exchanges building up ideas, I should push the student to attempt their own thesis based on what they've already articulated.

exchanges.student: maybe smth like corruption-free development is prob unrealistic, but treating corruption as culturally normal in Asia is also wrong bc cases like Hong Kong show institutions matter more than culture. i’m stuck on the last part tho about what corruption “signifies,” can u just tell me what that means in plain english?
exchanges.tutor: That thesis has real potential — you're making a clear argument. Now, on your question: think about it this way — if citizens routinely pay bribes to get basic services, what does that tell you about their relationship with the state? Do they trust it? Do they see it as legitimate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong thesis attempt and I should acknowledge that. They're now asking me to explain what corruption 'signifies' about the state and legitimacy — this is a key part of the prompt and I should use the Socratic method to help them arrive at the meaning themselves rather than explaining it to me. A targeted question will help them unpack it.

exchanges.student: yeah prob not — it suggests the state isn’t seen as fair or reliable, and ppl have to use informal workarounds. but fr can u just say plainly what “the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur” means? that phrase is so extra.
exchanges.tutor: I get that it's dense language! But think about it: what are some concrete things a state *does* to intervene in development — like, what tools or mechanisms does it use? Start there and the phrase might click.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good conceptual progress and connecting corruption to legitimacy well. They're now asking me to translate academic jargon for them, which is a reasonable struggle but something I should guide them toward rather than just explain. A question about what they think 'development interventions' means will help them unpack the phrase themselves.

exchanges.student: i think it means the whole set of methods the state and other actors use to actually carry out development — like bureaucracy, permits, budgets, contracts, monitoring, local officials, NGOs, aid systems, all that. can u check if that interpretation is right + make it sound less awkward?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that interpretation is solid. Now you have all the key building blocks: your thesis, the 'grease the wheel' critique, the Hong Kong counter-example, and what corruption signals about the state. It's time to stop analyzing in chat and start writing. Take a first crack at a full draft and bring it back — that's where the real work happens.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's interpretation is accurate and shows good understanding. I should affirm that without polishing their language for them — that's their job. We're also getting close to the point where they have enough conceptual material to start writing. Since we've covered the main ideas (grease the wheel critique, Hong Kong counter-example, state legitimacy, ensemble of techniques), I should push them toward integrating this into a written attempt.
